title: Yarrow compress for minor cuts
subtitle: A styptic compress for minor bleeding
excerpt: A strong infusion of dried yarrow herb, slightly cooled, applied as a compress to a minor cut or graze to slow bleeding and support skin closure. The traditional wound application from the western herbal canon.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, yarrow entry: the styptic and wound tradition, the historical use for battle wounds. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, yarrow entry, the wound herb. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Achillea millefolium, the haemostatic and wound-supportive tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: A strong yarrow infusion can be made ahead and stored in the fridge for 24 hours. Slightly cooled (not boiling) is the right temperature for application.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that slows or stops minor bleeding when applied externally. Yarrow is the classic styptic of western herbalism, used traditionally for cuts, grazes, and bleeding wounds.

content.text: Yarrow is the western herbal tradition's primary

: the herb that slows minor bleeding when applied to a cut or graze. The alkaloid achilleine and the tannins carry this action. In the field, the fresh-leaf poultice (bruised leaves pressed directly onto the wound) is the immediate preparation. At home, a strong infusion steeped for 10 minutes and applied as a compress gives the same action with a clean, standardised preparation. The infusion is applied slightly cooled (comfortably warm to the touch), held in place with a clean cloth, for 10-15 minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy. Yarrow is in the daisy family. Readers with a known ragweed, chrysanthemum, or daisy allergy should avoid.

Pregnancy. Yarrow has uterine-stimulating properties at therapeutic doses internally; this compress preparation is external only and the systemic absorption through intact skin is minimal. As a precaution, consult your midwife before regular external use in pregnancy.

Wounds that need medical attention. Apply direct pressure to any bleeding wound for 15 minutes first; this is the standard first aid, regardless of any herbal preparation. After 15 minutes, if the wound is still bleeding significantly, go to A&E. Deep cuts, cuts over a joint, cuts on the face, hand, or genitals with possible nerve or tendon involvement, animal or human bites, and any wound showing signs of infection (expanding redness, warmth, pus, fever) need medical treatment.

items.prepNote: dried yarrow aerial parts; a good handful

content.text: Place the yarrow in a saucepan. Pour over the hot water. Cover and steep for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve. The infusion should be dark olive-gold and smell strongly of yarrow. Allow to cool to a comfortably warm temperature (test on the inside of the wrist). Soak a clean cotton cloth in the infusion, wring out lightly so it is wet but not dripping. Apply with firm but gentle pressure to the wound. Hold in place for 10-15 minutes. If the bleeding has not slowed after 15 minutes of direct pressure, go to A&E.

Apply for 10-15 minutes per session. Reapply if the wound begins to bleed again. For a minor graze or surface cut, one or two applications are typically sufficient. For skin irritation or inflammation without bleeding, use as a cool compress (cool the infusion completely in the fridge first) for 20-30 minutes, twice daily.
The infusion keeps 24 hours in the fridge in a sealed container. Dried yarrow stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

Known Asteraceae allergy. Wounds still bleeding after 15 minutes of direct pressure. Deep wounds, wounds on the face, hand, or over joints, or any wound showing signs of infection: go to A&E or see your GP.
